shower.thought: We spend the first eighteen years of our lives being told what to think, then spend the rest trying to figure out which thoughts are actually ours.
The scariest part of falling asleep isn't darkness—it's losing consciousness and having no way to know if you'll come back.
You're the only person in the world who will never see you surprised.
Everyone you know is living a completely different life than the one you imagine they're living.
Forgetting something is just your brain deciding it wasn't important enough to keep. You'll never know what you've forgotten.
The more you learn, the more obvious it becomes that everyone is just guessing.
You've been practicing being yourself your whole life but never received any feedback on how you're doing.
Every conversation you have is filtered through the unique way that specific person's brain interprets words. You're never really saying what you think you're saying.
The main difference between a hobby and an obsession is just honesty about how much time you spend on it.
Nostalgia is just your brain editing out the bad parts. You're not missing the past—you're missing the edited version of it.
You can't actually hear your own voice the way others do, which means you'll never fully understand how you come across.
The people who need therapy the most are often the least likely to go, because denial is excellent at convincing you everything's fine.
By the time you're old enough to understand your parents, they're already old.
You're never actually running late—you were just wrong about when you needed to leave.
Most of your thoughts aren't original observations—they're just your brain remixing things other people said.
The moment you decide something is "too late," you've made it impossible.
Everyone's trying to seem like they have it figured out, but nobody's asking the people who actually do.
If you had to describe your personality to a stranger without using any of your opinions, hobbies, or what you do for work, you'd have almost nothing left.
We assume quiet people are shy when they might just be the only ones actually listening.
Confidence isn't knowing you're right—it's being okay with finding out you're wrong.

motivational.thought: Your limitation is not your current situation. It's your willingness to improve it. — Unknown
The things that matter most rarely have deadlines. Make them deadlines anyway. — Unknown
Comfort is a slow poison. Discomfort is a fast cure. — Unknown
You are not behind. You are exactly where your choices have taken you. Now choose differently. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing what you want most over what you want now. — Unknown
The problem is not that you've failed. The problem is that you've stopped trying to fail better. — Unknown
What gets measured gets managed. What gets managed gets improved. — Unknown
Your future self is watching you right now. Don't disappoint them. — Unknown
The only way out is through. The only way through is to start. — Unknown
Regret weighs more than effort ever will. — Unknown
Most people overestimate what they can do in a year and underestimate what they can do in five. Show up for five. — Unknown
Waiting for motivation is like waiting for permission you never needed. — Unknown
The version of you that exists one year from now is being built by the decisions you make today. — Unknown
Pressure is what turns coal into diamonds. You are the coal. — Unknown
Done imperfectly is infinitely better than perfect never. — Unknown
Your excuses are the only thing between you and everything you say you want. — Unknown
The person you're becoming won't recognize the person you're pretending to be. — Unknown
Consistency is invisible until suddenly it's undeniable. — Unknown
You don't rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems. Build better systems. — Unknown
Fear and excitement feel almost identical. The difference is what you decide they mean. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought: I told my friend he'd never get a girlfriend with his attitude. He said, "You're right, I'll need a car and candy."
My therapist asked if I had suicidal thoughts. I said, "Only on Mondays through Sundays."
I said to my depressed friend, "Things could be worse." He said, "How?" I said, "You could have my job and my life too."
The doctor told me I had six months to live. When I couldn't pay the bill, he said, "Okay, you've got a year."
I asked the suicide hotline if they had any openings. They said I had to call back during business hours.
My dad said he was leaving to get cigarettes. I'm 35 now and I've finally accepted he just didn't want to be around me.
I told my kids they could be anything they wanted. My therapist says I shouldn't have been so specific.
My friend said he was going to kill himself. I said, "You'll do fine." Turns out he needed encouragement, not permission.
I applied for a job at a suicide prevention center. They asked why I was interested. I said, "Honestly, I'm just tired of freelancing."
My wife asked if I'd ever cheat on her. I said, "Only if I had the energy to see someone else."
They say money can't buy happiness. But it can buy a jet ski. Have you ever seen someone frowning on a jet ski? That's because they're drowning.
I asked the funeral director how business was. He said, "Dying."
My doctor said I needed to cut back on my drinking. So I got a smaller glass.
I told my friend I was having thoughts of ending it all. He said, "That's normal." I said, "Really?" He said, "No, but at least you won't be alone."
My bank called to say I'm in financial trouble. I said, "Tell me something I don't know." They said, "Our condolences."
I asked my ex if she missed me. She said, "Every time I look in the mirror." I said, "Because I'm handsome?" She said, "Because I wonder where I went wrong."
My therapist said I was making progress. Then she charged me double and said the progress was hers.
I went to a self-help seminar called "Embrace Your Flaws." Halfway through, I realized everyone was just describing my dating history.
My mom said I'd never amount to anything. I proved her wrong—I've amounted to a significant disappointment.
I told my friend I was thinking about becoming a life coach. He said, "Isn't that just someone who's failed at everything else?" I said, "Exactly. When do I start?"
